meta.topic: One missing agreement can kill a deal — the IP assignment clause every founder misses
meta.audience: Founders who have or plan to hire developers, designers, or vendors

slides.label: Hook (0–8s)
slides.script: A founder I spoke to recently had a serious acquisition offer — call it a life-changing number. It fell apart in due diligence. The reason? The developer who built their core product — who'd left the company six months earlier — still technically owned the codebase. There was no IP assignment agreement.
slides.direction: Sushant direct-to-camera, talking head. Serious tone. No graphics needed — the story carries it.

slides.label: Explain the mechanism (8–25s)
slides.script: Here's how this happens. When you hire a developer — especially a freelancer or vendor — work they create is theirs by default unless there's a written agreement that says otherwise. Most founders use a simple offer letter or a WhatsApp conversation. Neither of those transfers IP. An IP assignment agreement is a single document that says: everything you build for this company, while engaged with this company, belongs to the company. It takes 15 minutes to draft. It takes months to fix when it's missing.
slides.direction: Still talking head. Can cut to a simple text graphic showing: 'Written agreement required to transfer IP' vs. 'WhatsApp message ≠ IP transfer'.

slides.label: Broaden it (25–45s)
slides.script: This isn't just about developers. It's your brand designer. Your CA who helped structure the company. Your co-founder who registered the domain in their personal name. Any of them can walk out the door holding something that belongs to your business — unless the agreement is in writing and it's specific. The three clauses that protect you: work-for-hire, confidentiality, and non-compete for the IP they created. You don't need a ₹5 lakh lawyer to draft these. You need someone to make sure they exist.
slides.direction: Slightly faster pace. Can show text overlays for 'work-for-hire / confidentiality / non-compete' as they're named.

slides.label: CTA (45–60s)
slides.script: If you've hired anyone — developer, designer, CA, vendor — and you don't have a signed IP assignment in place, reply to this and I'll tell you exactly what to ask for. At Envesty, we include this as a standard part of every company setup we run. It should be default for every founder.
slides.direction: Direct to camera. Personal, not promotional. Friendly urgency.

caption:
A founder I know had a serious acquisition offer fall apart in due diligence.

The reason: their developer — who'd left 6 months earlier — still technically owned the codebase. No IP assignment agreement.

This is more common than people realise.

When you hire someone — especially a freelancer or vendor — the work they create is legally theirs by default unless there's a written agreement that says otherwise. A WhatsApp conversation doesn't transfer IP. Neither does a basic offer letter.

An IP assignment agreement fixes this. It says: everything you build for this company, while working for this company, belongs to the company.

The three clauses you need in every employment and vendor agreement:
1. Work-for-hire (all output belongs to the company)
2. Confidentiality (they can't share what they built or learned)
3. IP non-compete (the specific assets they created aren't theirs to reuse)

You don't need an expensive lawyer to add these. You need someone to make sure they're there.

If you've hired anyone without a signed IP assignment in place — reply to this. I'll tell you exactly what to ask for.

#IPAssignment #IndianFounders #StartupLegal #BusinessSetup #FounderTips #StartupIndia
hooks: An acquisition offer fell apart because a developer who left 6 months ago still owned the codebase.
Most Indian founders don't have an IP assignment agreement with their developers. Here's what that costs you.
cta: If you've hired anyone without a signed IP assignment in place, reply to this — I'll tell you exactly what to ask for.
